Name: Consensus Mechanisms
Description: Distributed agreement protocols enabling trustless coordination - evolved from PoW to PoS variants. Ethereum has 800,000+ validators demonstrating decentralization.
Why D/acc: Enables trustless coordination without central authority; validator diversity prevents capture

Name: Data Availability Layers
Description: Decentralized storage for rollup data, enabling light node verification without downloading full blocks. DA costs represent 90%+ of L2 operating expenses.
Why D/acc: Decentralizes critical DA bottleneck, prevents validator centralization through Data Availability Sampling

Name: Settlement Layers
Description: Base layer blockchains providing final transaction validity and security. Ethereum has $48B+ L2 TVL, Bitcoin ~$25B annual security budget.
Why D/acc: Provides permissionless, censorship-resistant settlement without trusted parties

Name: Execution Environments
Description: Virtual machines for smart contract computation with varying performance/decentralization tradeoffs. EVM maintains 70%+ developer share.
Why D/acc: Open-source execution prevents proprietary lock-in; multiple VMs enable ecosystem redundancy
Key Players: EVM, Solana VM, Move VM (Aptos, Sui)

Name: Stablecoins
Description: Price-stable digital assets; $309B market cap, $27.6T annual volume exceeding Visa+Mastercard combined. 91% remain centralized.
Why D/acc: 91% centralized creates concentration risk; decentralized alternatives (DAI, Ethena) preserve utility with decentralization

Name: Lending/Borrowing
Description: Permissionless credit markets with ~$34B TVL. Flash loans enable uncollateralized borrowing within single transaction blocks.
Why D/acc: Removes gatekeepers from credit access; over-collateralization protects against bad actors

Name: Perpetual DEXs
Description: Decentralized derivatives with $1.4T+ annual volume (2024), up 872% YoY. Hyperliquid disrupted market to 64.8% share.
Why D/acc: Provides permissionless access to derivatives without centralized exchange counterparty risk

Name: AMMs
Description: Automated market makers enabling trustless token exchange. Uniswap V3 achieves up to 4000x capital efficiency vs V2.
Why D/acc: Removes reliance on centralized order books; intent-based systems redirect MEV to users
Key Players: Uniswap V3, Curve, CoW Protocol (63% intent trading)

Name: Decentralized Identity (DID)
Description: Self-sovereign identity with 200+ DID methods published. W3C standards maturing. Buenos Aires deployed ZK identity for 3.6M citizens.
Why D/acc: Creates self-sovereign alternatives to platform-controlled credentials; individuals own and selectively disclose identity

Name: Reputation/Sybil Resistance
Description: Multi-signal identity verification protecting $380M+ in funds with 2M+ users and 34M+ credentials via Gitcoin Passport.
Why D/acc: Enables verification of unique humanness without centralized authority; protects against coordinated manipulation

Name: Zero-Knowledge Identity
Description: Privacy-preserving credential verification - prove attributes without revealing information. Semaphore powers World ID.
Why D/acc: Directly counters 'with modern AI to interpret it, there may be no place to hide'

Name: Attestation Infrastructure
Description: On-chain credential issuance and verification. EAS enables 80% compliance cost reduction, 90% faster verification.
Why D/acc: Enables portable, verifiable credentials without reliance on centralized issuers

Name: Zero-Knowledge Proofs
Description: Verification without trust; $1.28B market (2024) projected to $7.59B by 2033 (22.1% CAGR). ZK L2 solutions: 60.7% CAGR to $90B by 2031.
Why D/acc: The only known technology allowing verification without trust, privacy without central authority, compliance without surveillance
Key Players: zkSync, StarkNet, Polygon zkEVM, Scroll, Aztec

Name: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)
Description: Computation on encrypted data. Zama achieved unicorn status ($130M). Current 20 TPS, targeting 1,000 TPS with ASIC hardware.
Why D/acc: Enables encrypted data processing, preventing AI systems from accessing raw information - the final frontier of on-chain privacy

Name: MPC (Multi-Party Computation)
Description: Distributed key management and computation across parties without revealing inputs.
Why D/acc: Eliminates single points of failure in key custody; enables cooperation without revealing sensitive data

Name: Voting Mechanisms
Description: On-chain governance with token-based, quadratic, conviction, and holographic consensus variants. Average participation only 17-28%.
Why D/acc: Enables broader participation in decisions; quadratic voting reduces plutocratic dominance through square-root weighting

Name: Treasury Management
Description: Streaming payments and DAO treasury infrastructure. $34B total DAO treasury holdings. Sablier pioneered token streaming.
Why D/acc: Enables real-time treasury flows without discrete approval bottlenecks

Name: Multisig Infrastructure
Description: Multi-signature authentication securing $100B+ in assets across 7M+ accounts with 30M+ transactions via Safe.
Why D/acc: Eliminates single points of failure in asset custody; enables social recovery without central custodians

Name: Quadratic Funding
Description: Mathematically amplifies small contributions; $60M+ distributed via Gitcoin, 400x matching multipliers demonstrated.
Why D/acc: Enables collective prioritization of public goods without centralized allocation

Name: Impact Certificates (Hypercerts)
Description: Tokenized impact claims (extended ERC-1155) with 10,000+ in circulation, 20,000+ unique addresses.
Why D/acc: Enables retroactive funding of public goods with verifiable impact

Name: Cross-Chain Bridges
Description: ~$1.4B TVL despite $2.8B+ historical exploit losses (40% of Web3 hacks). ZK-based bridges emerging as safer.
Why D/acc: Necessary for ecosystem connectivity but centralization tendencies create caution; ZK-based variants offer higher alignment

Name: Intent-Based Systems
Description: Solver competition for optimal execution. CoW Protocol commands 63% of intent trading with $2.4B monthly volume.
Why D/acc: Redirects MEV value back to users as price improvement

Name: Decentralized Storage
Description: Filecoin (22 exabytes capacity) vs Arweave ($9-12/GB for ~200-year permanent storage). 414% storage growth.
Why D/acc: Ensures data availability without platform dependency, preventing AI/corporations from controlling information access

Name: Oracle Infrastructure
Description: Off-chain data feeds. Chainlink 46-58% market share. Pyth grew 46x in 2024. Chronicle 61% cheaper operation.
Why D/acc: Critical middleware but market concentration creates d/acc tension; emerging networks with decentralized architecture preferred
Key Players: Chainlink ($20-39B TVS), Pyth ($4.7B TVS), Chronicle, RedStone

Name: Indexing
Description: Blockchain data queryability. The Graph covers 40+ blockchains with $3.4B GRT growth. Subsquid 100-1000x faster.
Why D/acc: Enables decentralized data access for dApps without centralized APIs

Name: Key Management
Description: MPC wallets, smart contract wallets (ERC-4337), social recovery. Combines MPC generation with smart contract features.
Why D/acc: Eliminates single points of failure; social recovery enables key restoration without central custodians

Name: Audit Mechanisms
Description: Security verification. $3.1B losses in H1 2025 alone. Immunefi paid $155M+ to whitehats. Code4rena warden model.
Why D/acc: Creates economic incentives for defensive security research
Key Players: Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, Spearbit, Immunefi, Code4rena

Name: Rollups
Description: Optimistic (83-85% share, 7-day finality) vs ZK (15-17%, instant validity proofs). ZK rollups growing rapidly.
Why D/acc: Scales blockchain without sacrificing decentralization; ZK rollups preserve privacy
Key Players: Arbitrum, Optimism, Base, zkSync, StarkNet, Scroll

Name: EIP-4844 / Danksharding
Description: Blob transactions (March 2024) with 10-100x fee reductions. Full Danksharding targets 100,000 TPS on rollups.
Why D/acc: Removes infrastructure bottleneck for decentralized scaling at protocol level
